commentBody: Hmmm am I missing something? Did I sleep through a day?? This should be the column for Wednesday’s column … not Thursday
The answer key is for Thursday’s puzzle. Wednesday - Cry after an accident? OOPSY

Oooh, what to do with the forbidden knowledge... 😈

"Honey, fastest xword solver cleans litterboxes for a week"
Party party party!!
Finished fast for me again. A good (or just easy?) week.
Now I gotta go back and find out how come I solved the puzzle unexpectedly.
I wasn't expecting a rebus today, so it was nice to see some energy in Damon's puzzle. I hope it wasn't too dark. Or obsure.
The lack of symmetry for the 'dark' theme squares is very off-putting.
This was delightful, and a very satisfying theme.

I predict a lot of complaints 

Me, I loved it. I did wish I could mark the squares I figured had to contain letters, which appear after solving but not before-- but it was a good Thursday. A little slower than my average, but not unpleasant.
Great puzzle!  I wonder if this was easier to solve on paper.  It was hard to keep track of the dark squares in my head.

I feel like the easy downs at the start gave away some of the fun.  It’s Thursday. Some things didn’t make sense. But I trusted the process. 

I think a great intro to Thursdays.  It will make you furious if you don’t know what’s coming. But you start to learn Thursday tricks. And this is definitely part of the toolbelt. 

Spent too much time trying to find my error. Reflexively entered SSn.  Given the cross is some Jewish? Month, didn’t notice anything.  

Speaking of which. Where’s that one dude that went crazy on Jewish stuff, about a month ago. It was like a drive by of loathing. 

Nice Thursday. Perfect for 2026 methinks.

For me it was a Wednesday, and Wednesday’s was a Thursday. 

L

I always enjoy when puzzles incorporate the black squares in the answers, so today’s puzzle was a treat for me.  I had a spot if trouble in the SE, because I had dark matter and wacky for a while, and couldn’t immediately make sense of ADV, but eventually straightened that mess out.

It was nice to see POORI get some crossword recognition of the sort usually reserved for ROTI and naan.

My cup runneth o'er!  With dark energy!

What a great Thursday puzzle.  Neat tricks with missing, excuse me, dark letters.  (Is that an anti-rebus?  Or would an anti-rebus square be left blank?  Or have negative entries?...ooh, I like that).

Anyway, an engraved invitation to talk about cosmology.  You guys are so lucky.

Dark Energy may be what most of the mass/energy of the universe is made of.  And we really don't know what it is.  And if we don't know what it is, it may not be real.  Most I can tell you is that whatever it is "dark energy" is, that's probably a bad name for it.

Werner Heisenberg of the famous Uncertainty Principle is said to have remarked that "Not only is the universe stranger than we think, it's stranger than we can think."

Dastardly clever. And cute that STAB is in here, as in "stab in the dark."  To come up with all the words needed, that work with and without each letter in ENERGY, and place them more or less symmetrically, have room for the revealer, AND have two super triple-stacks, that's some brio.

And anyone who hasn't seen Gloria Swanson's swan-song in Sunset Boulevard is in for a heartbreaking treat. 

This year I skipped Passover due to illness. Does that make me a Nisan Rogue?

And I like the various supporting kinds of dark energy in this puzzle: the Death Star is the manifestation of the Dark Side of the Force; idle gossip is a kind of dark energy, as the "devil's radio" alludes, fueling the destruction of reputations if not lives; Erykah Badu is frequently celebrated as a prime example of unapologetic, magnetic, fiercely confident "dark feminine energy," and molten obsidian sounds like some pretty dark energy.

Scarabs (a fancy word for dungbeetles) are decidedly not dark energy. In ancient Egypt, scarabs were prized as powerful magical amulets capable of resurrection, eternal regeneration, and divine protection. Because dung beetles rolled eggs in balls of dung—symbolizing the sun's daily cycle—they were ascribed the power of the sun god, who, unlike Amun Ra, Isis and Osiris, rarely appears in these grids: Khepri.
I think I found a new 'feature':  The crossword puzzle clock doesn't pause any more when I close the lid of my Chromebook like it did until recently.  Was this announced anywhere?
Breakthrough finally came when I realized the revealer was not a pun meant to be read "dark N or G."
Fun one!
Fresh and clever

Interesting trick. I caught onto it quickly, which was great help in filling the grid. However, I am slightly annoyed by how some of the letters revealed in the black squares once I solved the puzzle don't align with the letters in the light squares: https://imgur.com/a/ZBBZQa4 I learned from the puzzle the Supra is no longer made. I wanted to pick a nit with the grid and say how there is a current Supra model on the market (based on the BMW Z4), but apparently production stopped in March of this year. Very few of these recent Supras were offered in Europe, and thus they are an extremely rare sight on Warsaw's streets, strangely enough much more exotic than Ferraris and the like.

Finally, a proper Thursday!
Very artfully created, and a theme that snuck up on me. I had the two E’s first, so thought it was just E’s at first, then the R. Then I finally got to the revealer. That took a bit too as I had WEIRD first. Even when I got DARK I had DARKMATTER first, then DARKENERGY dawned on me. Even then I almost had an EPICFAIL because I wouldn’t give up WANKY and then WACKY. Finally got there!

This was a terrific construction. For those who didn’t like the asymmetry of the dark letters, you can’t have everything, and crossing entries that work both with and without the dark squares is difficult enough. A little glue here and there—obscure car brands, alternate spelling of flatbread, and even I try to avoid Jewish calendar months—but nothing too out of sorts for a Thursday or ungettable from crosses. 

Thanks, @Francis, I was counting on some erudite elucidation from you, although not much to be had. Your Heisenberg quote reminded me of the Feynman quote on quantum physics: “If you think you understand quantum physics, then you don’t understand quantum physics.”

This was a pretty easy puzzle, I worked out the theme very quickly BUT…. What a fine example of this kind of gimmick! All of the unclued fill worked perfectly as common words or expressions. I thought ESTAB was kinda weak, but at least that was the clued answer. I love a clean grid where the gimmick doesn’t leave any gobbledygook in the raw grid (unlike the recent “raising capital” puzzle)

I must have been in cosmic alignment and loved every minute of this slow burn, holding in my mind's eye the first 3 missing letters found (E, Y, G), figuring out that ENERGY would be part of the revealer, searching for the rest of the missing letters, keeping track in my mind of each one I found, and finally filling in DARK with a smile and an "aha!" Thank you, Damon, for the brain workout and for showing us what constructor chops are all about! My musical interlude for today comes via Led Zeppelin's SWAN SONG, an instrumental written by Jimmy Page. In the 80s, he founded the band The Firm, together with Paul Rodgers of Bad Company. Page then pulled SWAN SONG out of his back pocket and asked Rodgers to write lyrics for it. The result was "Midnight Moonlight": https://youtu.be/WaQ1gL_zgGQ?is=0cbAHK8h_Q-2NYL0
Oh boy. A gimmick puzzle (groan).
Did it properly with no rebus and got the prompt saying squares were incorrect. So I did it as a rebus. Still wrong. Deleted all the rebuses and then got message of success. Grrrr...

An excellent A.E. Thursday.

Internally-consistent gimmick?  Check.

Letters in the black squares spell out what the revealer says it will?  10-4.

Gimmick fill is actual verbiage and not gibberish?  Can happily confirm.

No minds blown.  Sadly, affirmative.  Fading quibble.

The last mind-blowing puzz is becoming a distant memory...

{cue the Wayne's World flashback hand fluttering and "diddly-doodly-do-diddly-doodly-do"}
For all those who wished to try rebuses, remember that the have to work in some way in both directions,  So, entering (RE) at the end if 1A, would give 9D commencing (RE) also, ruling RAIN out

Despite giving this one a DEATH STARE, I couldn't see the DARK ENERGY and suffered an EPIC FAIL. I think I will adjourn to the CANTIna and have some ENCHILADAS and ICED coffee. LEDA and SWAN SONG in the same puzzle. Nice. Puzzle adjacent songs of the day Dark Star and Southern Cross--Crosby, Stills and Nash https://youtu.be/vEzc8GNI6-I?si=nDjxu7apkzpDf7de https://youtu.be/HNosjGDx2uQ?si=-yKHCXR4EvwnlWp7
Clever
I really enjoyed this puzzle!

Wacko is one of my favorite words, so useful in describing so many situations, people, and in this case, a puzzle. I made a godawful (another fave) mess of it, until I read the revealer clue to my husband, and he said DARKENERGY.  Oh. Got it.  Went back and did some housecleaning (it was not that kind of rebus) and the whole puzzle blossomed. 

I loved it Damon. What a clever and idea, and what a delight to solve (after my better half solved the better half of it with that one phrase). Thank you!
For more DARKENERGY see the puzzle from July 26, 2015, description and solution found here: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/26/2015

Third degree Nerd Alert:

There's not much to say about Dark Energy.  Or Dark Matter, for that matter (see what I did there?).  

Actually that's not really true, and even if it were, it wouldn't stop me from saying just a little bit about *why* we think there is missing energy/matter in the universe.

Think about our solar system, for a moment. Sun at the center, Mercury, Venus, Earth, etc.  The planets nearer the Sun orbit the faster--Mercury screams around the sun every couple three earth months.  Pluto, whether a planet or a dwarf planet, takes 250 earth years to get all the way around.

But if you try to think of all the suns in a galaxy kind of acting like a big complicated solar system, it works in some ways and fails in another.

It is true that galaxies rotate around a center.  But it rotates more as a solid body, like the spokes of a bicycle, rather than like the solar system.

This gets quite involved, but basically scientists came to the realization that all the matter we could see in the universe (stars, planets) didn't add up to enough mass to make the galaxies behave as they do.

So there's something missing.  And that's what we call Dark Matter and Dark Energy.
Disappointed it wasn't black holes.
Did I really just solve a Thursday puzzle with no help from the Wordplay column? It must be a very easy one. A fun one, though. Enjoyable.

Was going nowhere in this one before nailing my first “dark matter” (the G). There’s something so satisfying about working out what’s going on in a puzzle and 3 or 4 doors instantly opening in front of you! I really enjoyed this one!
Nice, easy Thursday. Reckon we're gonna get punished in the next two days 😬🤣
Shouldn’t that be Ark Nergy? I mean, if it means what it says.
That was a fun, clever grid.

This one wasn’t quite so satisfying for me. I got the trick early on in the top right, so I was looking for the hidden always to be at the ends of the crossbar of the dark “T”—which they were until they weren’t.

But I appreciated that the words that appeared in the grid were real words/phrases even without the darkened letter. And it took me about my average time, which I also appreciated.

I don’t know the construction term, but the missing letters were all the black squares that were extra in both directions. 
I had DARKMATTER stacked onto BONECHINA to start with. Quite a mess! Fun puzzle.
Excellent puzzle! Nice twist!
That was tricky, but great fun!

I definitely had some trouble starting off with 'epic moment' instead of defeat and 'no rules' instead of 'no score'. I Thought this was going to be a rebus, so I was trying to fit in 'dark entropy' before energy. Once I realized the errors in my ways and the trick clicked for me, a good time was had! I think this would have been a good puzzle for paper and pencil to keep track of all the dark matter.

Took a while, but SWAN SONG 
cracked “it” open,

tho I hadn’t gotten to the revealer,

so it was fun to figure out…
what it was…

that I needed to figure out.

Oi. 


Sometimes I say things
and I can hear it.

The silent, implied declaration 
that I am an unapologetic 

word nerd.
As my Gen Zs say: this is big dark energy!
Loved the baseball clues today about A-Rod, AAA and a home run TROT. Although I wish the clue was about Trot Nixon and 2004 Red Sox to counter the A-Rod clue. Go Sox!

After Damon’s fun and clever puzzle, I inexplicably made a list of the dozen un-clued answers, took it as a challenge to place them in an ode, and here it is:


Ode To Mr. Swanson

THIN Mrs. SWANSON
With a DEATH STARE, but otherwise
A face UNREAD
QUIPPED
“My strong as an APE but
Normally taciturn SPOUSE, after Spain’s world Cup victory
AMBLED in ASCENT up our rock-climb wall to the roof
Took a STAB, and
Amid our lovely quiet neighborhood, shrieked ‘OLE!’ again and again into the night
I may never leave the DEN”
Love, love, love!!!!

Dark energy:

It’s [not just any]  [unnamed article].

It’s THE THING.

I never heard the term DARK ENERGY, because that's just not my thing. Nevertheless, I got the trick very early and found the puzzle to be very easy. In fact, easier than yesterday's. I did like the way the letters in the gray boxes on my paper copy went in a sort-of order, so I avoided having to figure out an anagram. Clever, but IMO, still not Thursday-worthy.
That was fun (so much better than a rebus, which I don't like)
Not sure how this is fair. The squares are black to indicate that there isn't a letter in that square. This puzzle is going in the bin.

A genuine Thursday puzzle. Thanks! 

Caught on at (R)OLE/(R)AMBLED, and then the rest was much easier. Excellent theme and revealer.

Took me about 25 minutes, but worth every minute. I'll even survive the overdose of 3-letter abbreviations and initialisms all starting with A: ADV, ACL, AAA, ALS, AGR.

I've noticed that a clue like LONG STRETCH, which iimmediately suggests EON to me, is now taking ERA as well. That delayed me in the southeast corner for a while.

One of the best aha moments of the year!  Made me go ash, not ahh!
Try again:  One of the best aha moments of the year!  Made me go aah, not ahh!
Since when are we supposed to place letters in the darkened squares? This seems unnecessarily tricky.

This was my first Thursday crossword so I was nervous to say the least. It was pretty hard for me, especially with the hidden letters. Still super fun though, I can't wait to get better like some of you pros in here!

Whew. And Wow. Typical tough Thursday for me, but finally tumbling to the trick was just a huge turning point and that's always a nice touch. And... thought it was just amazing that all of the theme answers were valid words or phrases even without the hidden letter (though of course not matching the clues). 
DEATHSTAR
SPOUSE
ASCENT 
APE 
QUIPPED 
STABBED 
etc. etc. 

Wow. 

And of course a puzzle find today. I'll put that in a reply. 

....

Damon likes to play around with words.  For instance, in his last puzzle, about a year ago, the theme was SPEECH TO TEXT, where the clues had the kind of errors that we often see in computer translations.  My favorite was the clue for EIFFEL TOWER – [Rod-shaped parasite] – where “Paris site” was misheard.

Today he played around with letters in the black squares, even having them spell something.  Also highly creative was his clue for ADV – [Wholly, but not holey or holy, e.g.: Abbr.].   

ADV or ADVERB is almost always clued directly, such as [Adj. modifier], or by giving adverb examples, such as [Now or never].  But Damon’s clue today takes it to a level not seen before in any of the major crossword venues, involving rhyme and contrast.  Brilliant out-of-the-box thinking, and it made a dull answer special.  

Bravo on the above, Damon. It was fun to untangle your excellently executed theme, and afterward, to learn post-solve about dark energy.  Thank you for this!

While doing this puzzle on my phone I found myself wishing I could put letters in the black squares. This would be easy to do on paper, but allowing it in the app would give away the gimmick.

This got me thinking- how much are gimmicks limited by needing to work in the app? Are there any past gimmick puzzles created when they were paper only that are difficult or impossible to solve in the app, or did the NYT never get that adventurous? For example I'm thinking of things like having symbols or other non-letter things in answers, or going outside the grid.

Although this isn't related to the main puzzle, there is no comments section for the "Midi", so if it's okay with the powers that be, I'm going to leave my comment here. 

I just wanted to give a ton of thanks to Ian Livingood for the hysterically funny and clever clue to 2-Down in today's Midi. I chuckled for an absurdly long time over it, mostly thinking that it would probably fly under many solver's radar (and maybe the Eds.?).

Despite my pet name for this constructor (i.e., Damon the Demon), I was delighted to see the byline.  Every so often I dip into his thoroughly enjoyable book of rejected puzzles (complete with commentary and solutions conveniently on the next page, if one is moved to cheat.)  It is a tiny bit too big to go with me to MD appts, (hint for next time, Damon!) but it lives on my dining-room table, close at hand...

Oh, THIS puzzle.  It did take me slightly longer to catch what I was meant to do with the missing/spare letter, but eventually I ran accross the Reveal and it helped me accept  that not every puzzling puzzle entry needs a shoe-horn or two letters in a box, or what-all.

I am in a weakened condition, (PT will be after breakfast, ugh; yesterday a slew of lab orders)...  gosh, the fun never stops when you reach A Certain Age.  A few of these clues were downright cruel:  "Letters before ".gov".... Srsly?  We could be here all day!

I wanted VOLCANIC fr "obsidian"...all LAVA is MOLTEN at one time, but it certainly doesn't always produce the fancy stuff...Oh, well.  The -OL- was correct, at least.

Y'all have a good day and mind your doctors.

The answer for 46A brought to my mind an episode of Rod Serling’s “The Twilight Zone”;  “Queen of the Nile” (Season 5, Episode 23).  No doubt many of you are old enough to have had the privilege of seeing this episode; not to mention all the other brilliant ones during the series run between 1959 and 1964.  For the rest of you, there is — or at least was at one time — the complete DVD set of this series.  Can’t recommend the series highly enough.
Gotta be that guy that points out that mathematically, a sphere is not a solid but a surface. The correct term here would be a ball.

Maybe it was getting up early for the cats, who knows! But my xword senses picked up on the theme once I clicked into the northeast and I was off to the races. Very nice couple of back-to-back lovelies this week. Thanks, constructors and editors!

How good is this?!  The missing letters work in both the Acrosses and Downs; they spell out DARK ENERGY in order; and, taken away, they leave real words in both the Acrosses and Downs!

There's even a dastardly trap set for us on top -- and I fell right into it.  Having figured out the theme, more or less, at DEATH STAR, I was simply looking for a real word that, with a letter added, would be the answer to the clue.  The answer was obviously NAPE and so, with great confidence, I wrote in NAP.  

But (brilliant trap really!) the answer is APE.  Because it's not the last letter that gets removed as in DEATH STAR, but the letter that's in the black square and the one that's needed to spell out DARK ENERGY.  And once I had that, I knew the revealer would be DARK ENERGY.  Which was a big help with THING/SWAN SONG and UNREADY/DENY.

Are there a lot of three-letter answers needed to make the theme work.  Yes, and who cares?  This is very clever, very well-executed, and great fun to work out.  It's going into my running list for POY nominations.  Kudos, Damon!

tricky, tricky, but I got through without peeking at the Wordplay column. I had "prepped" before I knew there was a hidden "e" to make it "equipped," but got it eventually. Nice how the dark energy letters magically appear at the end.
Loved this! Excellent puzzle that made me think. More please!
As a relative noob (<3 months of trying) to NYT crosswords, I found this one very hard. Harder than your average Thursday. “Nisan” was particularly obscure.
Barely enough breadcrumbs to find my way.  But I found the ENERGY to push on.  Can't DENY the cleverness of this puzzle.  Kudos to Damon Gulczynski!

It is absolutely *fascinating* to me how a black square containing an extra letter seems to bother fewer people than a white square containing an extra letter.

End of observation, carry on!

For 26A - To paraphrase Mitch Hedberg: 

Toyota used to make Supras.  They still do, but they used to too.

I spent way too long trying to fit in every slightly sporty bygone Toyota model that I could think of before I realized that the cluse was actually looking for one of the two sports cars that they are still making.

Other than that, fun puzzle.  I enjoyed the theme.  Maybe a bit easy for a Thursday, but it was well executed.

I saw all the right answers, but did not know I was supposed to put answers on black squares - entered them as rebuses instead.

Does the puzzle allow you to put letters on black squares? not normally.
Tripped up by the technicality of how to enter, even though saw every answer

Recognized the theme as soon as I hit the revealer (although it's been a little while since we've seen it here) and quickly had all but the G. Took stepping away and coming back to realize it could only be a black square in a very specific configuration. Fortunately there were only two of those (with only two sides free) between the R and the Y. Changing THIs to THIN[G] made that whole area of the puzzle make a lot more sense.

And the theme words without their dark energy were also real words. This puzzle made the physics, language, and puzzle parts of my brain all happy together.

What was the picture that showed up over the puzzle at the end?  And I wish it would show you the "missing" letters because I forgot all of them as soon as both related answers were in.  Which, incidentally, makes it much harder to do these sorts of puzzles.

I went from BLACK HOLES to DARK MATTER to DARK ENERGY … I‘ve never heard of dark energy before and would have preferred either of the other words as revealer but the fact that the six black squares spell ENERGY totally makes up for this. So fun, really well done!

Poor old Aethelred the UNREADY, the original "spare." He became king at a very young age, when his brother, Edward the Martyr, was assassinated, and had to deal with the Danes straight away. He did so poorly, and ended up having to pay them off. Top of mind, because he was an answer on Jeopardy! the other night.
I get that this is a puzzle. But is it still a crossword puzzle when the letters are imaginary and go into blank spaces. The reference to dark energy was cute, but imaginary letters?
I am not surprised that the Toyota Supra, especially the $60,000 variety, is no longer being produced.  Never in all my travels have I seen one of those on the road or in a parking lot.
Awesome Wednesday puzzle!

And... my usual late puzzle find. A Tuesday from March 26, 1996 by J.S. Marrone. Four fifteen letter grid-spanning answers in that one: THERITEOFSPRING SUMMERINTHECITY AUTUMNINNEWYORK THELIONINWINTER Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/26/1996&h=27a ..
Horrendously. A week of bad offerings. Shame.

The NYT crossword has jumped the shark. 
I always liked that expression. It derives from a moment late in the run of the "Happy Days" TV series. Out of ideas, the writers have The Fonz go water-skiing, in his leather jacket. A shark shows up, and he jumps it. 
If you look up the definition of "crossword" you'll not find what we saw today. Letters that don't exist in the white squares but are somehow to be imagined as existing in the black squares is not a crossword. 
Now, some may say I'm a fuddy-duddy, a crossword conservative who brooks no changes from the norm. Kinda? 
I think the art of a *good* novel crossword is in providing a twist that makes sense in some interesting and compelling way. 
A great successful example was the puzzle that had "Ship of Theseus" as an answer, then various alterations answer by answer and eventually, "Ship of Theseus" as the last in the series. Strictly speaking, one should not see the same answer twice. But it made sense and illustrated the thought experiment. Knowing some bits about the thought experiment, one could solve the puzzle. 
There isn't anything about "dark energy" that logically leads to "put letters into the dark squares." 
It's gotten to the point where constructors and the editors approving the puzzle are effectively saying "if you have a theme answer that has a term like 'dark,' 'missing,' 'gone,' 'double' etc. you can justify anything in the grid." 
Feh. 
I really like my morning crossword. Bring it back, for I am sad.
Oh darn. You tricked me.
Years of listening to Sean Carroll podcasts made the cosmology reference an instant fill.

I don't always get the Thursday NYT crossword without a hint or two.Today I got it with no help.  I enjoyed the trickery. 
Bravo to the creator.
Not a good day for my first NYT puzzle. I didn't know you could just break the rules of a crossword. Well that's a frustrating end to a frustrating hour of toil.

I don't know. The puzzle was ok, but the missing (dark) letters could just as well have been "regular" and I would have liked it better. It just seemed like the constructor was trying too hard to include a trick that didn't really need to be included.

I recall the first time I completed a puzzle with hidden letters. I think they were literally off the grid in that case, but that's not important to my comment.

It was several years ago, but I was even then a very experienced solver. At this point in time I've been solving for close to 50 years; then it was somewhere between 40 and 45. 

I had to refer to the column, and even then was challenged to get the theme. This was probably mostly because I was simply outraged that I was expected to figure out that certain letters simply were not IN the grid! I wasn't ready or able to let this idea into my crossword brain. 

Once my outrage settled to minor irritation, I rethought the whole experience and realized the fun and genius of it. Fast forward to today, and I immediately grasped that some letters were hiding behind dark squares. I needed the revealer to fully understand and appreciate the theme, but  did take the time to appreciate how my solving skills continue to develop even at my advanced age! (66. . . Not really THAT advanced, but still. . . )

In short, I loved the puzzle, appreciated the "trick", and completed it slightly under my average Thursday time.

I was shocked when I got an instant solve upon entering the last letter. I typically struggle a bit more on Thursdays. Luckily, I got the first half of the revealer (DARK) straight away which tipped me off that the theme contained hidden letters vs rebus.

Wow! I loved this puzzle. It took about five minutes over my average but that's OK. I actually laughed when the added letters appeared at the end. Surprised? Disappointed? That so many comments here were negative. Good work Damon!

Lots of fun. I like puzzles with hidden letters.
Congrats Damon!

Fun puzzle!   I caught on with missing letters with DEATH STAR(E).   I missed out on a gold star by having trouble with one area.   If I had noticed at blurry-o'clock last night that the missing letters were to spell "energy", that would have helped.  But I enjoyed the challenge.

I had DEATHSTAR(E) all worked out, and then I added (N)APE, so I was off and running.

I also had DARKMATTER confidently filled in. 

"Clever!" I thought. "The letters are dark, just like the MATTER."

(I figured it out eventually.)

Anyone else see the “ERG” in the revealer in a sort of slash from mid upper left ?  I guess common etymology with energy from ergon.

Erg a regular crossword visitor.

Is it dark energy or… newly understood changes in the evolution of supernovae?  Probably still dark energy, but self-correction in science is a great virtue.

The great Enrico Fermi even got a Nobel for what in part was wrong… he overlooked the possibility of nuclear fission.  Ida Noddack tried to correct him but wasn’t heeded:

“Ausenium (atomic symbol Ao) and Hesperium (atomic symbol Es) were the names initially assigned to the transuranic elements with atomic numbers 93 and 94, respectively. The discovery of the elements, now discredited, was made by Enrico Fermi and a team of scientists at the University of Rome in 1934.”

Eventually Hahn, Meitner, and Frisch cleared it up.  Not Oppenheimer.
My commitment to "ironstone" had me boxed in. It wasn't until I deleted those letters that I saw how to rework and complete.  Anyone else have a similar experience?
What a pointless game has?  I didn’t get the pun at first.  Really appreciated that clueing.
Very fun Thursday. Over my average time, so I feel like my brain stretched a bit, which is always a good thing. Thanks, Damon!

Some people like to tell other people to look in the sky and to see how inconsequential we are in comparison. Here's one example from "Carousel:" Billy: [spoken] I don’t need you or anyone to help me. I got it figgered out for myself. We ain’t important. What are we? A couple of specks of nothin’. Look up there. I absolutely loved this. Perfectly difficult, IMO, and a fun, tight theme. Even if it did remind me that it's only Thursday. Sigh.

I loved the theme! It took a while to figure it out and even when I did I was wrong about the theme word. At first I assumed BLACK HOLE but that didn’t fit, then I REALLY wanted it to be DARKMATTER and it fit (but boy did it cause me problems later)… However, this helped me with the tricky 6 areas that needed an extra square…


I hated the deceptive clueing: 41A Will you let me? CANT I? It works but a little cheap IMO. There were also a lot of very difficult answers (which is completely fine) but when it is in a gimmicky puzzle with plenty of its own tricks, it becomes a little too much (again, IMO)

Rating:
7/10

Really excellent puzzle today. Just the right level of crunchiness and cluing that challenged me, but when I finished, I wanted another.

Okay, I have to give a rare thumbs down.  Although it was clever, (upon seeing the final result) I had to rely on a number of look-ups to even approach getting entries to make sense.  The missing letter feature caused me to plug in quite a few wrong words.  Some of the cluing was more than a little vague as well.  Congrats and a tip o' the cap to anyone who completed it without assistance.
Very cool! I thought ADV was the hardest:)

Great puzzle, though another average killer.  Definitely enjoyed the theme and trick.

I continued to SPOUSE working with Downs and eventually AMBLED into the 62A "gimme" DARKmatter. Luckily being wrong didn't "matter" as I immediately grasped the black square idea (QUIPPED by having seen it before). And from there completed bit by flyspecking bit.  Thanks for a fun puzzle.
That was a real piece of work.
Great crossword to puzzle out as I'm riding through Montana today  (not driving!) ...you can feel the cosmic enery everywhere you look... Big Sky so aptly named!  Grazie Damon!

Completely disagree with all the haters. I really enjoyed this one and the gimmick. And if you don't like Thursday rebuses/gimmicks, at least it wasn't a single random square with a whole word in it on a Wednesday like yesterday. Took me longer than usual but it wasn't unfair or anything.
That was hard—but FUN!

Nice theme. Having DARK matter instead of ENERGY for the revealer held me up for a long time. I didn't notice that the "dark" letters spelled out ENERGY before solving and then reading this column; that might have sped things up.

Ah!  Regularly tackling the NYT's Spelling Bee paid off today in the Xword.  I knew 30 had to be RO4, oops I mean ROTI!

The meaning of AMBLE is to go at or as if at an easy gait : saunter. a leisurely walk. We ambled along as we talked. They ambled down the road... So to have clues for words that sound like the word is clever? Way over my head.

Lots of fun, the kind of Thursday I love. I wouldn’t want them every day but I always look forward to Thursdays. And since I blew my little streak yesterday starting a new one with this fun one.

Didn’t we see almost this exact theme (dark matter as the revealer and similar construction) a few years ago???? (I’m thinking of 2/2/23, also a Thursday and one of my favorite puzzles). Are we just recycling things now?

Thankfully, I'm a physics nerd and picked up DARKENERGY on the first run.  I actually changed it to DARKMATTER at one point but the crosses helped.  Cool Thursday gimmick.  I, for one, look forward to these.

Mathematical pedantry note regarding 46D: a sphere is, by it's formal mathematical definition, not a SOLID. A sphere is defined as the collection of points a uniform distance from a given center point. All the stuff inside that shell of points is technically not part of a sphere. The mathematically correct term for everything inside a sphere is a "ball."
Very fun and engaging! Reminds me of the DARKHORSE Thursday puzzle from a few months ago.

But this was definitely an innovative theme.

@Vaer

I was down and out in Istanbul in 1984, traveling, writing, working odd jobs for train fare and notebooks.  Staying in a roach-infested youth hostel and down to my last few lira.  Was seriously considering packing it all in, going back home to the States and getting a job.
A fellow traveler with a guitar, playing in the common room while I lay sweat-drenched and sleepless in my dorm bunk, wailed the sweetest cover of Southern Cross I have ever heard, before or since.  He followed it up with Little Feat's "Willin'."  Made me shake and quietly weep joyful tears for being alive and free and among such beautiful people in such a breathtaking city.  

I remained abroad two more years.

Thanks for jogging this memory, Vaer.

@Jazmine
Both spellings are correct in anglicized Hebrew. I started with APRIL there, which of course *can* be the month of Passover, but NISAN (or NISSAN, if that's a Saturday, =confusing, clue) works better, even without the crosses, because Passover always falls in the month of NISAN/NISSAN.

@Adam

Agreed.  TBH the letter in the black square bothers me a lot more simply because I have to keep remembering it on every pass through the grid, and cannot just write it in.

@Adam I think it's that it's a one-time thing, and it's seen as creative. Rebuses have been around for decades. A lot of the complaints on Wednesday were because the rebus appeared on that day, which threw people off.

There are fewer comments with this type of puzzle because (a) newbies don't need to ask how to put more than one letter in a square and (b) there are no issues about which letters must be entered and what entry order is required. Also, the revealer reveals all. There have been other harder "black letter" puzzles that caused quite a bit of pushback.

Adam,

Alternate theory: the repulsive force of this trick was somehow even stronger than a rebus, and people ended their attempt not with a nasty comment, but in a whimper.

@George, Thursday is one of the worst days to start on the NYT crossword puzzles. They are, along with Friday and Saturday, usually the toughest. Start with Monday and you'll have much better luck and probably more fun.
Thursday is trick day, Friday and Saturday are tough, and Sunday is unpredictable. Monday is a good day to start.
@George Nixon, the "rules" of crosswords are quite fluid, actually. You may need an update user manual! Don't be discouraged.

@George Nixon
Welcome aboard. Thursday NYT CWPs normally have a think-way-outside-the-box gimmick like this. Most solvers who stick with Thursdays come to love the puzzles, though it's often not love at first sight. So I get your reaction, but I suggest rolling with it for a while, reading Wordplay as needed, and keeping an open mind to new challenges. A few people decide to skip Thursdays altogether.
